blocks.content: Davenport had been clear about what he needed: availability, honesty, patience. What he hadn't said was what they were supposed to do with themselves in the meantime. So they did what people do when there are no instructions — filled the hours with small things and tried not to look at the room upstairs.

blocks.speaker: Sophie
blocks.content: I called my office. They think I'm dealing with a family emergency.

blocks.speaker: Sophie
blocks.content: My boss asked how long I'd need. I said I didn't know. He said to take the time I need. I think he assumed it was something with my parents.

Dean nodded. He'd had a similar conversation with his own office that morning on the porch. The same vagueness, the same letting of assumptions go uncorrected. There was no version of the truth that fit comfortably into a normal conversation.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: My clients are going to notice I'm gone by Wednesday.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: I'll figure something out. It's not the most complicated thing I'm dealing with right now.
Wren came in from the porch and poured herself a glass of water and stood at the counter drinking it.

blocks.speaker: Wren
blocks.content: Has anyone spoken to Marcus's other friends. Outside this group.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: I called his mother. And his colleague James — they worked closely. James is handling the office.

blocks.speaker: Dean
blocks.content: He was shocked. He said Marcus had seemed fine at work. Normal. No signs of anything.

blocks.speaker: Wren
blocks.content: Everyone keeps saying that. He seemed fine.

blocks.speaker: Wren
blocks.content: I wonder what he was actually feeling.
